response: The internet traces its origins to 1960s U.S. Department of Defense-funded computer networking research: its precursor ARPANET was first demonstrated in 1969 with a message from UCLA to the Stanford Research Institute, and 1983’s transition to TCP/IP protocols established the modern internet’s technical backbone. A critical accessibility milestone arrived in 1989 via Tim Berners-Lee’s CERN-based invention of the World Wide Web (encompassing HTML, URLs, and HTTP), followed by 1993’s Mosaic browser, which made the web accessible to ordinary, non-research users. Subsequent key shifts included the late 1990s dot-com boom (and 2001 crash that left survivors like Amazon and Google to become global corporate leaders), mid-2000s social media platforms (Facebook, Twitter, YouTube) redefining communication, 2006’s AWS launch spurring cloud computing, and the 2007 iPhone catalyzing ubiquitous mobile internet. Today, the internet connects over 5 billion people worldwide and underpins virtually every facet of modern life, from commerce and communication to entertainment.
